core_concepts: Mechanical Tension as the Primary Driver: Hypertrophy is fundamentally an adaptation to tension. Maximizing tension requires handling challenging loads through a full range of motion.
Progressive Overload: The necessity of consistently increasing mechanical demand over time (weight, reps, or sets) to force further adaptation.
Volume Thresholds: The target range of 10–20 effective sets per muscle group per week provides the highest return on investment for most lifters.
Proximity to Failure: Effective sets must be taken within 1–3 Reps in Reserve (RIR) to ensure recruitment of high-threshold motor units.
Nutritional Foundation: Protein (1.6–2.2g/kg) is the essential building block; without it, the stimulus provided by training cannot be fully realized.

actionable_advice.sets_and_reps: Perform 10–20 working sets per muscle group weekly. Rep ranges can vary (5–30), but 6–12 is efficient for balancing load and metabolic stress.
actionable_advice.frequency: Distribute weekly volume across 2–3 sessions per muscle group to optimize the muscle protein synthesis response.
actionable_advice.intensity: Train close to failure (1–3 RIR). If a set doesn't feel challenging enough to prevent an additional 1-3 clean reps, the intensity is likely too low.
actionable_advice.progression_methods: Implement 'Double Progression': stick to a weight until the top of the rep range is reached for all sets, then increase the load by the smallest increment possible.
actionable_advice.recovery_advice: Cap volume at 20 sets per muscle weekly. If strength stalls or persistent soreness occurs, initiate a deload week (reducing volume by 50%).
actionable_advice.nutrition: Prioritize 0.7–1.0 grams of protein per pound of body weight, spread across 3–5 feedings to optimize protein synthesis.

scientific_claims.claim: Mechanical tension is the undisputed primary trigger for muscle protein synthesis (mTOR activation).
Volume follows an inverted-U curve; beyond 20 sets per muscle/week, recovery capacity is often outpaced by fatigue.
Training muscles 2+ times per week is superior to 1x per week for long-term hypertrophy.

coaching_insights: The 'Logbook Rule': If you aren't tracking load and reps, you are just exercising, not training for growth. Growth requires objective data.
Quality vs. Quantity: One set taken to 1 RIR is worth more than three sets taken to 5+ RIR.
Movement Quality: Range of motion is a component of tension. A half-rep with 200lbs provides less tension to the target tissue than a full-rep with 180lbs.
common_mistakes: Junk Volume: Performing many sets with very low intensity, thinking that 'more time in the gym' equals 'more muscle.'
Program Hopping: Switching exercises every week, preventing the body from mastering the movement and achieving true progressive overload.
Ignoring Protein: Relying on the stimulus of training while failing to provide the amino acids required for repair.
Ego Lifting: Using momentum and partial range of motion to handle weights that the target muscle cannot actually control.

instagram_posts: Why your logbook is your best training partner.
How to set up Double Progression.
Stop wasting time with Junk Volume.
RIR: How to know if you're working hard enough.
youtube_shorts: How many sets do you REALLY need?
Stop ego lifting: Fix your ROM.
Is your rest time too short?
motivational_quotes: Mechanical tension is the currency of growth.
If you aren't tracking, you aren't training.
Progressive overload is the law of the gym.
Build the stimulus, then feed the recovery.
One hard set beats three easy ones.
Master the motion, then push the weight.
Volume is the tool, tension is the goal.
Adaptation is a choice, not a given.
myths_addressed: Myth: You need 'muscle confusion' to grow.
Myth: You MUST train to absolute failure every set.
Myth: 1 set per exercise is sufficient.
Myth: Protein timing matters more than daily totals.
Myth: Lifting heavy compounds is only for powerlifters.
Myth: You can 'shape' a muscle with high reps.
Myth: Soreness is the only indicator of a good workout.
Myth: Machines are inferior to free weights.
Myth: You need more than 20 sets per week.
Myth: Cardio kills all muscle gains.

grit_application.goal: Optimize volume management and progressive overload through automated tracking.
grit_application.routine: Create a 'Weekly Volume Dashboard' that flags when users hit the 10–20 set threshold. Prevent exceeding 20 sets to avoid systemic overtraining.
grit_application.instruction: Implement a 'Tension Check' prompt: 'Did you use full ROM?' and 'Was this challenging?' after every logged set to encourage quality control.
grit_application.tracking: Sync nutrition data to ensure protein intake hits the 0.7g-1.0g/lb target, pushing alerts if daily protein dips below the maintenance floor.
